content.text: Data Contract Breach Detection and Remediation

A data contract breach occurs the moment any monitored SLA term crosses its threshold: completeness drops below 99%, freshness exceeds 24 hours at the 95th percentile, a duplicate appears in a supposedly deduplicated batch. Detection must be automated and near-real-time because a RAG pipeline consuming stale or incomplete data silently degrades answer quality without raising any application-level error. Remediation must be systematic — the same breach handled inconsistently on two consecutive days erodes producer credibility and forces consumers to build defensive logic everywhere.

attrs.alt: Data contract breach detection and remediation flow

content.text: A breach is any measured value that violates a contracted threshold, regardless of how small the delta. Thresholds exist precisely to draw a clear line between acceptable and unacceptable quality. Common breach triggers:
Source system outage, ETL null coercion
Schema change upstream drops a field
New label added to source before contract updated
> 4 h from source commit to index
The classes below model a breach event through its lifecycle, from detection to closure:

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import time


class BreachSeverity(Enum):
    P1 = "P1"   # critical — >5× threshold or >25% data missing
    P2 = "P2"   # moderate — 2–5× threshold
    P3 = "P3"   # minor   — 1–2× threshold


class RemediationAction(Enum):
    RE_DELIVER   = "re_deliver"   # producer re-sends the affected batch
    BACKFILL     = "backfill"     # producer patches historical window
    FIELD_PATCH  = "field_patch"  # targeted correction for null/invalid fields
    ESCALATE     = "escalate"     # manual intervention required
    ACKNOWLEDGE  = "acknowledge"  # P3 — logged, no automated action


@dataclass
class BreachEvent:
    contract_id: str
    term_type: str        # "freshness", "completeness", "null_rate", ...
    threshold: float
    actual: float
    detected_at: float    # unix timestamp
    severity: BreachSeverity
    batch_id: Optional[str] = None

    @classmethod
    def from_violation(cls, violation, contract_id: str, batch_id: Optional[str] = None):
        ratio = violation.actual / max(violation.term.threshold, 1e-9)
        if ratio > 5 or (violation.term.term_type.value == "completeness"
                         and violation.actual < 0.75):
            sev = BreachSeverity.P1
        elif ratio > 2:
            sev = BreachSeverity.P2
        else:
            sev = BreachSeverity.P3
        return cls(
            contract_id=contract_id,
            term_type=violation.term.term_type.value,
            threshold=violation.term.threshold,
            actual=violation.actual,
            detected_at=time.time(),
            severity=sev,
            batch_id=batch_id,
        )

    def elapsed_seconds(self) -> float:
        return time.time() - self.detected_at

The registry tracks which breach dimensions are currently active for each contract. It deduplicates: if a freshness breach fires every 15 minutes, only the first event opens a record — subsequent firings do not create duplicate pages or Slack alerts.
The handler orchestrates the full lifecycle: receive a monitor result, open new breach records, dispatch notifications, trigger remediation, and close breaches once the monitor clears.
Each action maps to a concrete integration — a replay API call, a backfill job trigger, or an on-call page:

Keep remediation actions idempotent. Re-delivery and backfill jobs will be triggered again if the handler restarts before the breach registry is persisted. Design each action so that running it twice produces the same outcome as running it once.
The ratio of actual to threshold determines severity, which drives the notification and response tier:

P1 breaches with completeness < 75% are always critical regardless of ratio because a RAG index that is missing more than 25% of its chunks cannot be trusted to answer accurately — serving answers from it causes confident hallucinations.
A well-designed consumer does not wait for the breach to be resolved — it degrades gracefully as soon as it receives a breach notification. The
pattern below switches between live, cached, and suppressed modes based on the registry state:
Wiring Detection, Handling, and Fallback Together

# Wire everything together at startup
registry = BreachRegistry()
handler  = ContractBreachHandler("hr-policy-docs-rag-v1", registry)

# In the ingestion pipeline — called for every arriving batch:
def on_batch_received(batch: list[dict], batch_id: str, ingestion_latency_hours: float):
    monitor = SLAMonitor(contract)
    result  = monitor.check(batch, ingestion_latency_hours)
    handler.handle(result, batch_id=batch_id)

# In the consumer RAG pipeline:
retriever = FallbackRAGRetriever(live, cache, registry, "hr-policy-docs-rag-v1")
answer    = retriever.retrieve("What is the parental leave policy?")
if answer["mode"] != "live":
    print(f"⚠ {answer.get('warning') or answer.get('error')}")
Every P1 and P2 breach requires a post-mortem filed within 48 hours of resolution. The post-mortem document captures:

: exact breach start and end time, time to detection, time to first notification, time to resolution. Measured against the contracted breach notification SLA (≤ 15 min) and incident response SLA (P1: ≤ 1 h).

: the specific upstream change or failure that caused the breach — not "data pipeline issue." E.g.: "The embedding service was restarted without draining the ingest queue, causing 847 records to be re-enqueued with duplicate chunk IDs."
: which consumer pipelines received breach notifications, whether they activated fallback, and whether any end-user requests were served from stale or suppressed data during the window.
: the concrete fix applied and the preventive measure that prevents recurrence — e.g., an idempotency key added to the retry logic and a duplicate-rate check added to the pre-ingest validator.

: if the breach revealed an unrealistic SLA threshold, note it as a candidate for renegotiation in the next contract review cycle. Do not silently relax the threshold in the YAML without following the MAJOR bump and 30-day notice process.
deduplication, a breach that lasts 4 hours and is checked every 15 minutes generates 16 identical pages. Track open/close state, not just violation events.
: consumers that activate fallback mode need to be told when the breach is resolved so they can return to live data. The
: if the producer publishes micro-batches every 60 seconds, checking freshness only at end-of-day misses a 23-hour drift window. Match monitor cadence to the producer's delivery frequency.
Threshold set to the current average
: a completeness threshold of 0.998 when the current average is 0.999 gives no buffer. Any fluctuation triggers a page. Set thresholds at the
A breach is any measurement that crosses a contracted threshold — even a 0.001 exceedance is a breach if the threshold is 0.0.
Classify severity by the ratio of actual to threshold: >5× is P1, 2–5× is P2, 1–2× is P3.
The BreachRegistry deduplicates open breach records so a 4-hour breach generates one notification, not one every 15 minutes.
ContractBreachHandler maps each SLA term type to a default remediation action (re-deliver, backfill, field-patch, escalate).
Consumer-side FallbackRAGRetriever reads the registry and degrades to cached or suppressed mode automatically on breach notification.
P1 and P2 breaches require a post-mortem within 48 hours covering timeline, root cause, consumer impact, and corrective action.
Never silently relax a threshold after a breach — follow the MAJOR bump and 30-day notice process for any SLA change.
